item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I remember correctly, while I was critting this the author mentioned that the Ad Astra reference was intentional and referring to that canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just wanted to point out that you are excellent when it comes to clinical writing.

I don't know how you did it, but you made me care for an inanimate object that isn't even sentient. Good work all around.

As an aside, I have to wonder: would this article work in the Ad Astra Per Aspera canon? The narrative places importance on facilitating and maintaining off-world travel, and even if the inclusion of a program titled "Ad Astra" is coincidental, the interactions between the Persons of Interest and the anomaly share thematic threads with what you'd expect from that canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of scips solely rely on their execution as articles, which triggers the "narrative investment" thing and makes them work as scips. However I don't think this one could even possibly be just an anomalous object, due to its orbital nature along with the fact that instances eventually get big enough for their self-replicating nature to be worthy of interest in canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why it needed to be sentient for me to regard it as a skip, and that puzzles me. There are plenty of non-sentient skips. Maybe it is a narrative investment thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't understand that the skip was sentient the first time I read through it.

I think I failed to notice the one sentence about audio being spliced together from clips.

I went "Wait, what? That's it? Seems like an anomalous object to me, not a skip"

Read it a second time.

"Oops. OK it is sentient. I suppose that's unusual enough to be a full fledged skip."

Then followed fruitless contemplation on why the quality of having sentience made my brain classify it that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is… good. Very good. It's short yet it me hard and right in the feels. That's an upvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These satellites are so adorable! They have love, families, and they help clean up space debris!

They are amazing! Well, until a fleck of paint hurtling through low-orbit at over 17,000 miles-per-hour comes and hits one of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best part about this for me is the oddity of it, and its relative innocence as an SCP. Just a cute little orbital trash collector! The audio files are also pretty adorkable, because they're so self-indulgent and don't really need to be in the article, but "mom" and "dad" are clearly AWARE that they're self-indulgent, which makes it an adorkably parental thing to do.

I think it could use a bit more polish on the description section — there's a lot of "it does this," "it does that," that doesn't sound particularly scientific and could use some shortening. Nothing egregious and probably just a taste thing for me.

Strangely, the final spliced audio showing the SCP-4954's sentience is the piece that doesn't really sell for me, despite how much I loved SCP-2886 doing similar things with spliced audio. It's a nice poem, but doesn't feel like an emerging or alien consciousness wrote it, which kills the suspension of disbelief for me. Rather than deepening the mystery or introducing a new wrinkle to the plot, however minor, this approach ties the whole article off with a neat little bow and doesn't make me think any further about it.

My suggestion would be to review that final poem and compare to the final sections of 079, 1000, and 2886 — those are my favorite examples of how other minds attempt to communicate with humans. This version feels a little too contrived in comparison.

Good luck, space roomba.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I could have maybe expanded more on the creators and what they did to give better depth to the machines. I mostly just focused on the imagery of the space roombas knowingly sacrificing themselves although if their sentience isn't well expressed it does damper the emotional aspect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a cute little piece, and it's nice they have somebody working on the space junk problem, but this doesn't really do anything for me. The technology isn't elaborated enough to make it interesting, and the emotional angle doesn't accomplish much, since the level of sentience (if any) in these devices is not clear, and I don't have an investment in the characters.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to gee0765 and TheBlueHour for their crit! Oh, and the Sanskrit character used in the task force is pronounced "Saa" if anyone is wondering.
